prompt: The only mammal that can truly fly
hostIntro: Three choices. One is right. Two will make you look dumb. The only mammal that can truly fly — go.
hostCorrect: Bat! Flying squirrels glide. Flying fish jump. Only the bat actually flaps its wings and flies. You knew that.
hostWrong: Bat! Flying squirrels just glide — they're basically furry paper planes. Flying fish aren't mammals. Bats are the real deal.
hostTimeout: Bat. The only true flying mammal. Flying squirrels are liars with skin flaps.

hostIntro: Three oceans. One is the biggest. Which one? Atlantic, Pacific, Indian — pick.
hostCorrect: Pacific! It covers more than a third of Earth's surface. It makes the Atlantic look like a puddle.
hostWrong: Pacific! The Atlantic is the second largest. The Indian Ocean is third. The Pacific is so big it has its own Ring of Fire.
hostTimeout: Pacific Ocean. Over 165 million square kilometers. You could fit every continent inside it and still have room.

hostIntro: Only one of these is a Shakespeare play. Hamlet, Hercules, or Horatio. They all start with H. That's where the similarities end.
hostCorrect: Hamlet! To answer or not to answer — you answered. Well done. Horatio is a character IN Hamlet, and Hercules is Greek mythology.
hostWrong: Hamlet! To be, or not to be — well, it's definitely Hamlet. Horatio is Hamlet's friend, not a play. Hercules is Greek mythology.
hostTimeout: Hamlet. The most famous play in the English language. 'Alas poor Yorick, I knew him, and apparently none of you do.'

prompt: The first element on the periodic table
hostIntro: The periodic table starts with... Helium, Hydrogen, or Carbon? One of these is atomic number one.
hostCorrect: Hydrogen! Element number one. The most abundant element in the universe. Makes up the sun. Pretty important.
hostWrong: Hydrogen! Helium is number 2, Carbon is number 6. Hydrogen is first. It's literally in the name — hydro, water — actually it's in water. Never mind.
hostTimeout: Hydrogen. Atomic number 1. It makes up stars, water, and the first page of every chemistry textbook ever.

hostIntro: One of these is Japan's currency. Won, Yen, or Yuan. All three start with a Y sound. Only one is correct.
hostCorrect: Yen! Japan uses the yen. Won is South Korea. Yuan is China. They're easy to mix up. You didn't.
hostWrong: Yen! The Japanese yen. Won is South Korean. Yuan is Chinese. All sound similar, all very different things.
hostTimeout: Yen. Japan's currency since 1871. Won is Korea, Yuan is China. Geography matters.

prompt: The country with the most time zones
hostIntro: Which country has the most time zones? Russia, China, or France? Think carefully. This one has a trick.
hostCorrect: France! France has 12 time zones thanks to its overseas territories around the world. Russia has 11. China technically has one (it's complicated).
hostWrong: France! Surprising, right? Thanks to its overseas territories like French Guiana, Martinique, and French Polynesia, France spans 12 time zones. Russia has 11.
hostTimeout: France. 12 time zones. Because of its overseas territories spread across the globe. Russia has 11, China uses just one official time zone despite its size.

hostIntro: Chemistry time. The chemical symbol for gold is... Ag, Au, or Gd? One is gold, two are not.
hostCorrect: Au! From the Latin 'aurum', meaning gold. Ag is silver, Gd is gadolinium. Au is the one that costs $2,000 an ounce.
hostWrong: Au! From the Latin 'aurum'. Ag is silver from 'argentum'. Gd is gadolinium, which is less glamorous. Au is your gold answer.
hostTimeout: Au. Latin: aurum. The chemical symbol for gold. Ag is silver. This is why Latin class matters, people.

prompt: A word that means extremely happy
hostIntro: Three words. Only one means extremely happy. Elated, Elevated, or Elaborated. Sound it out.
hostCorrect: Elated! Over the moon, on cloud nine, thrilled to pieces. That's elated. You should be feeling it right now.
hostWrong: Elated! It means extremely happy and joyful. Elevated means raised up. Elaborated means explained in detail. Elated is the feeling.
hostTimeout: Elated. Meaning extremely happy. The opposite of what this room is right now.

prompt: The most widely spoken language in the world
hostIntro: The most widely spoken language in the world — by number of native speakers. Spanish, English, or Mandarin?
hostCorrect: Mandarin Chinese! Over 900 million native speakers. English is second by some measures, but by native speakers, Mandarin wins by a landslide.
hostWrong: Mandarin Chinese! Nearly a billion native speakers. English has more total speakers if you count second language, but Mandarin wins on native speakers.
hostTimeout: Mandarin. The language of about 1 in 6 people on Earth. You might want to start learning.

prompt: The phobia of clowns is called...
hostIntro: A lot of people have this fear but don't know the technical name. The phobia of clowns is called... Coulrophobia, Clownophobia, or Bosomania?
hostCorrect: Coulrophobia! From the Greek 'kolobathristes' — stilt walkers. Because ancient Greeks also didn't trust people on stilts in face paint.
hostWrong: Coulrophobia! 'Clownophobia' sounds right but isn't a real clinical term. 'Bosomania' was made up just now. Coulrophobia is the one.
hostTimeout: Coulrophobia. The irrational fear of clowns. After this game, you may develop a new one.

prompt: The planet with the most moons
hostIntro: The planet with the most confirmed moons. Jupiter, Saturn, or Uranus? The answer changed recently.
hostCorrect: Saturn! As of recent discoveries, Saturn has over 140 confirmed moons, edging out Jupiter. Space is still being explored.
hostWrong: Saturn! It recently overtook Jupiter with newly discovered moons. Over 140 confirmed. Uranus is a distant third. Sorry, Jupiter.
hostTimeout: Saturn. With over 140 confirmed moons as of recent discoveries. Jupiter used to hold the record but Saturn took it back.

prompt: The smallest planet in the solar system
hostIntro: Which planet is the smallest in our solar system? Mars, Mercury, or Neptune?
hostCorrect: Mercury! It's smaller than some moons. Don't feel bad for it though — it's extremely close to the sun and has no real atmosphere. It's fine.
hostWrong: Mercury! Mars is about twice Mercury's size. Neptune is a giant. Mercury is the tiny one that spins right next to the sun.
hostTimeout: Mercury. The smallest planet. Also the fastest orbit and the most extreme temperature swings. Small but chaotic.
